metadata.contributionModel: Public GitHub pull requests against a single curriculum JSON

tracks.title: Computer Science Foundations
tracks.tagline: A complete CS1 path for learning programming from first principles.
tracks.description: An original Python-first introductory computer science course built around skills, lessons, checks, and spaced review.

tracks.title: Machine Learning Foundations
tracks.tagline: A math-ready first course in modeling, prediction, evaluation, and responsible ML practice.
tracks.description: An original ML1 course for learners who already have the programming and mathematics needed to focus directly on machine learning.

courses.title: CS1: Introduction to Programming with Python
courses.tagline: From program state to functions, collections, files, testing, algorithms, recursion, and objects.
courses.description: A standard first-semester CS1 course for beginners. The course emphasizes reading code, tracing state, decomposing problems, testing behavior, and building small Python programs with confidence.

courses.title: ML1: Machine Learning Foundations
courses.tagline: Modeling, prediction, evaluation, and responsible ML practice for math-ready learners.
courses.description: A first machine-learning course for learners who already have the needed math background and want to focus directly on ML concepts, model behavior, and practical workflow.

modules.title: Program State and Execution
modules.summary: Learn how Python programs run, store values, evaluate expressions, and communicate through the console.

modules.title: Conditions and Choice
modules.summary: Use booleans, comparisons, and branches to make programs react to data.

modules.title: Iteration and Patterns
modules.summary: Master repeated execution with while loops, for loops, accumulators, sentinels, and nested loops.

modules.title: Functions and Decomposition
modules.summary: Break programs into named pieces with parameters, return values, scope, and small designs.

modules.title: Strings and Text Processing
modules.summary: Treat text as structured data with indexing, slicing, searching, and parsing patterns.

modules.title: Lists and Sequence Processing
modules.summary: Use lists and sequence traversal to store, search, transform, and summarize many values.

modules.title: Dictionaries and Structured Data
modules.summary: Represent lookup tables, counts, groups, and simple records with dictionaries and nested data.

modules.title: Files and Data Summaries
modules.summary: Move from small examples to real inputs by reading files, parsing rows, and reporting summaries.

modules.title: Testing and Debugging
modules.summary: Read errors, design useful tests, and isolate bugs with evidence instead of guesses.

modules.title: Robust Programs
modules.summary: Handle bad input, expected failures, and edge cases without hiding defects.

modules.title: Algorithms and Efficiency
modules.summary: Recognize simple algorithms and compare how much work different approaches do.

modules.title: Recursion
modules.summary: Understand recursive definitions through base cases, smaller inputs, and traceable returns.

modules.title: Objects and Integration
modules.summary: Use simple classes for state and plan a small multi-function program from requirements.

modules.title: Problem Framing
modules.summary: Turn real-world questions into learnable tasks with examples, features, labels, and clear evaluation goals.

modules.title: Data and Features
modules.summary: Prepare machine-learning datasets while avoiding leakage, representation mistakes, and misleading data quality assumptions.

modules.title: Linear Regression and Optimization
modules.summary: Use linear models, loss functions, gradients, and scaling to understand supervised prediction.

modules.title: Classification and Metrics
modules.summary: Predict classes, choose thresholds, and evaluate classifier behavior beyond raw accuracy.

modules.title: Generalization and Model Selection
modules.summary: Diagnose underfitting and overfitting, use validation correctly, and select models without fooling yourself.

modules.title: Tree Models and Ensembles
modules.summary: Reason about decision trees, their failure modes, and why ensembles often improve stability.

modules.title: Unsupervised Learning
modules.summary: Find structure without labels using clustering, dimensionality reduction, and anomaly detection.

modules.title: Neural Network Basics
modules.summary: Understand neurons, layers, activations, and training at the level needed for a first ML course.

modules.title: Practical ML Workflow
modules.summary: Connect preprocessing, pipelines, model comparison, and experiment records into a repeatable workflow.

modules.title: Responsible ML Projects
modules.summary: Plan end-to-end ML work while accounting for bias, drift, monitoring, and communication.

skills.title: Diagnose overfitting and underfitting

skills.title: Explain regularization effects

skills.title: Use cross-validation
skills.description: Can describe cross-validation workflow and when it is useful.

skills.title: Plan hyperparameter searches

skills.title: Control tree complexity

skills.description: Can explain why ensembles can improve stability and when they reduce interpretability.

skills.title: Select anomaly detection

skills.title: Map network layers

skills.description: Can order preprocessing and estimator steps in a repeatable ML workflow.

skills.title: Plan end-to-end ML projects
skills.description: Can outline the essential pieces of a responsible first ML project.

lessons.title: Read sequential programs
lessons.summary: Program order matters. Python executes one statement, updates state or output, then moves to the next statement unless control flow says otherwise.
lessons.objective: Trace a short Python script from first line to last line and predict its visible output.

sections.title: Core idea
body: Program order matters. Python executes one statement, updates state or output, then moves to the next statement unless control flow says otherwise.
Program order matters. Python executes one statement, updates state or output, then moves to the next statement unless control flow says otherwise.
sections.checkpoint: Which value or state change determines the next step?

sections.title: Reasoning method
body: Start with one concrete input or state. Write down intermediate values before deciding the final result.
Compare what each statement reads, what it changes, and whether it returns or prints a value.
sections.checkpoint: What intermediate value would catch the most common mistake here?

workedExamples.prompt: print("ready"); print("go")
steps: The first print statement displays ready.
The second print statement displays go.
Nothing runs out of order unless the code explicitly changes control flow.

assessments.title: Entry diagnostic
assessments.description: Checks early execution, state, branch, loop, and debugging readiness.

items.prompt: Which statement best describes sequential program flow?
options: The computer randomly picks a suitable line
The computer executes instructions in a defined order
The computer only executes the last line
items.correctAnswer: The computer executes instructions in a defined order
items.explanation: A sequential program follows the written execution order unless control flow changes it.

items.prompt: `x = 2`; then `x = x + 5`. What is the final value of `x`?
items.explanation: The old value 2 is read, 5 is added, and 7 is stored back into x.

items.prompt: If `score = 12` and the condition is `score > 10`, which branch runs?
options: The true branch
The false branch
Neither branch
items.correctAnswer: The true branch
items.explanation: 12 is greater than 10, so the condition is true.

items.prompt: `total = 0`; then a loop adds `1`, `2`, and `3`. What is the final value of `total`?
What is the best first move when a program gives the wrong answer?
options: Change many lines at once
Reproduce the bug and inspect the relevant state
Delete the whole module
items.correctAnswer: Reproduce the bug and inspect the relevant state
items.explanation: A reproducible case and visible state make debugging evidence-based.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Read sequential programs
assessments.description: Checks whether the learner can apply read sequential program flow.

items.prompt: Which habit best supports read sequential program flow?
options: Trace concrete values step by step
Guess from variable names only
Change unrelated code until output changes
items.correctAnswer: Trace concrete values step by step
items.explanation: Concrete tracing keeps reasoning tied to actual execution.

items.prompt: What should you record when predicting the next statement?
items.explanation: Recording state or values makes each step checkable.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Track variable state
assessments.description: Checks whether assignments can be read as state changes.

items.prompt: `coins = 4`; then `coins = coins + 2`. What is the final value?
items.explanation: 4 is read, 2 is added, and 6 is stored.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly describes `score = score + 1`?
options: An invalid equation
The variable value increases by 1
The variable is deleted
items.correctAnswer: The variable value increases by 1
items.explanation: The old value is read, one is added, and the result is stored again.
